verification.note: No Income Tax Act section governs alimony directly; treatment rests on judicial doctrine (principally Bombay HC, Princess Maheshwari Devi of Pratapgarh v. CIT (1984) 147 ITR 258) and is consistently described the same way across current tax-advisory sources. Well-established for straightforward facts; whether a specific 'lump sum' is a genuine full-and-final settlement (capital receipt) versus a disguised periodic arrangement (revenue receipt) is a fact-specific judgment call that needs CA review of the actual decree/deed.

source_refs: Princess Maheshwari Devi of Pratapgarh v. CIT, (1984) 147 ITR 258 (Bombay High Court) — cited via BCAJ and TheLeagle secondary analyses, primary judgment text not directly retrieved
Income Tax Act, 1961, Section 56 (Income from Other Sources)
Taxation of Alimony, Sunil Shenoy, Bombay Chartered Accountants' Society Journal, https://bcajonline.org/journal/taxation-of-alimony/
When A Princess Worried About Tax on Alimony, TheLeagle, https://theleagle.in/?p=1056
Is Alimony In India Taxable?, ClearTax, https://cleartax.in/s/alimony-india-taxable
Lump-Sum Alimony Not Taxable While Monthly Maintenance Is Taxable, TaxGuru, https://taxguru.in/income-tax/lump-sum-alimony-taxable-monthly-maintenance-taxable.html
Alimony & Taxation: Is Divorce Settlement Money Taxable in India?, Tax2win, https://tax2win.in/guide/alimony-taxation-divorce-settlement-india

lump_sum_settlement.basis: Judicial doctrine (Princess Maheshwari Devi of Pratapgarh v. CIT, 1984), not a statutory exemption; only applies where the lump sum genuinely extinguishes future maintenance rights as a full-and-final settlement, not where it functions as a bundled/accelerated periodic payment
